sample.title: The Midnight Sky
sample.plot: A scientist is working alone at a remote Arctic research station when he learns of a mysterious global disaster. At the same time, he meets a strange girl in the Arctic, with whom he must stop the return to Earth of the crew of the spaceship "Aether".

categories.label: research stations at the end of the world
categories.description: Human presence on the polar continent depends on small, isolated stations built to keep people alive amid lethal cold, distance, and darkness. What you'll see: sealed corridors, generator rooms, ration stores, sleeping bunks, and windows looking out onto endless snow and ice.

categories.label: survival resting on fragile life-support
categories.description: In this frozen world, heat, power, air, medicine, and communications are not conveniences but the thin barrier between life and death. What you'll see: failing equipment, improvised repairs, fuel worries, battery checks, and every task measured against the cold outside.

categories.label: science outposts linked by sparse signals
categories.description: Knowledge and safety depend on radio traffic, satellite relays, and scattered listening posts rather than any dense web of settlements. What you'll see: antenna arrays, control consoles, static-filled transmissions, weather reports, and long waits for answers that may never come.

categories.label: a ruined Earth shadowing daily life
categories.description: The polar station exists under the knowledge that the wider world has suffered a vast calamity, leaving ordinary routines haunted by absence and uncertainty. What you'll see: abandoned rooms, emergency notices, empty transport links, and the stillness of a place built for many but inhabited by few.

categories.label: space travel tied to earthly command
categories.description: Even in a future of deep-space voyages, ships remain dependent on guidance, tracking, and decisions from distant people and instruments on Earth. What you'll see: flight readouts, orbital calculations, docking procedures, mission logs, and anxious efforts to reach crews across immense distances.

categories.label: machines extending human reach into ice
categories.description: Travel, observation, and rescue in Antarctica rely on vehicles and instruments that let people move through terrain no body can master alone. What you'll see: tracked carriers, aircraft, survival suits, medical scanners, navigation screens, and lamps cutting through whiteout conditions.

categories.label: small crews carrying heavy responsibility
categories.description: Because the environment permits only limited habitation, a few specialists must bear the work of science, maintenance, judgment, and care for others. What you'll see: lone rounds through the station, checklists, medical triage, technical troubleshooting, and silence broken by duty calls.

categories.label: the polar sky as warning and wonder
categories.description: Above the ice, the heavens are both a realm of beauty and a source of danger, tying local survival to events far beyond the continent. What you'll see: auroral light, star-filled darkness, radiation fears, horizon watching, and the contrast between cosmic grandeur and human frailty.

categories.label: distance making solitude a daily condition
categories.description: On this continent, remoteness is not occasional but the basic shape of life, stretching every errand, message, and hope of rescue. What you'll see: empty snowfields, long interior walks, muffled footsteps, solitary meals, and the oppressive quiet of a world with almost no neighbours.
